agent_raw: The AutoML insurance claim investigation benchmark cannot be accepted for implementation. Two independent contamination paths inflate the apparent AutoML advantage. First, target encoding for high-cardinality categorical fields (repair shop, policy channel, assessor ID) and median imputation were applied to the full two-year modeling table before cross-validation fold creation. Cross-validation is only a valid performance estimator when all outcome-dependent transformations — including target encoding — are computed within each training fold. Applying target encoding globally allows each fold's held-out outcomes to influence the feature values used in that fold's training data through the shared encoding statistics. This is a standard preprocessing leakage error. Second, the operating threshold was selected on the held-out test set to achieve 80% recall. Even though 80% recall is an operational requirement, the specific threshold achieving it was identified using test data, making the reported precision at that threshold an in-sample estimate rather than a true holdout result. Additionally, the logistic baseline retains its original threshold from a prior project with a different preprocessing protocol, making the comparison asymmetric — the AutoML system benefits from two protocol advantages the baseline does not have. The steering committee should not proceed with implementation based on this benchmark. A corrected evaluation with fold-specific target encoding, validation-set threshold selection, and matched protocols for both systems is required.
all_issues_raised: Full-dataset target encoding before fold creation — leakage inflates CV AUC
Threshold selected on test set — precision estimate contaminated
Baseline uses different preprocessing and threshold protocol — asymmetric comparison
